gotchas.title: Denali road is closed at Mile 43
gotchas.body: The Pretty Rocks landslide severed the park road in 2021. Buses run only to East Fork (Mile 43); Eielson and Wonder Lake are off-limits by road until a bridge opens (~2027). Plan around it.

gotchas.title: Denali bus tickets sell out
gotchas.body: You cannot drive the park road yourself past Mile 15 — you ride a park bus. Reserve at recreation.gov the moment tickets open (early winter for summer). Walk-up seats are scarce.

gotchas.title: Brooks Falls needs a float plane + a permit-tight camp
gotchas.body: Getting to Katmai means a jet to King Salmon then a float plane to Brooks (~$700-900 round trip). Brooks Lodge beds go by lottery a year and a half out; most people day-trip.

gotchas.title: McNeil River bears are a lottery
gotchas.body: The world's best brown-bear viewing is a state permit drawn each March for a handful of summer slots. Apply early; most years you won't win.

gotchas.title: Pack Creek needs a permit too
gotchas.body: The Admiralty Island bear sanctuary caps daily visitors June-Sep. Book the permit and your float plane together before you fly to Juneau.

gotchas.title: Rental cars ban gravel highways
gotchas.body: Most contracts forbid the Dalton, Denali Hwy, McCarthy Rd and Nabesna Rd, and won't cover a cracked windshield. Rent from a company that allows them (or a specialist) if you're going north.

gotchas.title: The Whittier tunnel runs on a clock
gotchas.body: The 2.5-mile one-lane tunnel alternates direction and shares the bore with the train. Miss your window and you wait 30-60 min. Check the schedule before you commit to a Sound cruise time.

gotchas.title: Ferries book up and reschedule
gotchas.body: The Alaska Marine Highway sells cabins months ahead in summer and revises sailings when a vessel goes to refit. Book early, build slack, and check your sailing the week before.

gotchas.title: Bush flights cancel for weather
gotchas.body: Small planes to Katmai, Lake Clark, Gates of the Arctic and the like scrub for fog and wind. Never schedule a fly-in on your last day — leave a buffer day or you miss your flight home.

gotchas.title: You can't drive to Juneau
gotchas.body: The capital, plus Sitka, Ketchikan, Kodiak, Nome and Cordova, have no road connection. Southeast towns are air or ferry only — plan those legs as flights, not drives.

gotchas.title: Store your food; this is bear country
gotchas.body: Use bear-proof lockers, never leave food in reach, carry spray and know how to use it, and keep 100 yards from any bear. On salmon streams give them the trail.

gotchas.title: Cell coverage dies fast
gotchas.body: Outside towns and the main highway corridors there is no signal. On the Dalton, McCarthy Rd, Denali Hwy and most of the bush, download maps and tell someone your plan.

gotchas.title: Fuel gaps are real
gotchas.body: The Dalton has fuel only at Yukon River, Coldfoot and Deadhorse. On remote highways fill up at every chance — running dry out here is a genuine emergency.

gotchas.title: Gravel eats tires and windshields
gotchas.body: Carry a full-size spare (two on the Dalton), drive slow on washboard, and pull right for oncoming trucks to save your glass. A rock star can strand you 200 miles from help.

gotchas.title: Turnagain Arm mudflats can kill
gotchas.body: The glacial silt off the Seward Hwy is quicksand and the bore tide comes in fast. Look, photograph, but don't walk out onto the flats.

gotchas.title: The midnight sun wrecks your sleep
gotchas.body: In June it never really gets dark. Bring an eye mask, book rooms with blackout curtains, and don't be surprised when kids won't sleep at 11pm.

gotchas.title: Mosquitoes are the state bird
gotchas.body: Late June through July, especially in the Interior and Arctic, they're relentless. DEET or picaridin, a head net for the bush, long sleeves. They ease off by mid-August.

gotchas.title: Cold water is the real hazard
gotchas.body: Alaska water runs 35-50°F year-round. A capsize or a slip means hypothermia in minutes. Wear the life jacket on every boat; dress in layers you can add and shed.

gotchas.title: Cruise-port days are short
gotchas.body: Ships dock ~8am-5pm. Excursions that fly or boat far out cut it fine. Book the cruise line's own tours for the far ones so the ship waits if you're late.

gotchas.title: Fishing needs a license and has limits
gotchas.body: Buy a sport license online before you cast. Bag limits are strict and king (Chinook) salmon are often closed to protect the run — check the current emergency orders.

gotchas.title: Drones are banned in the national parks
gotchas.body: Launching, landing or operating a drone in Denali, Katmai, Glacier Bay and the rest is illegal. Great for harassment complaints, terrible for your wallet.

gotchas.title: There's no statewide sales tax — but boroughs add their own
gotchas.body: Alaska has no state sales tax, yet many towns levy local sales + bed taxes (Juneau, Ketchikan, etc.). Anchorage has none. Budgets vary town to town.

gotchas.title: Tip your guides
gotchas.body: Fishing captains, bush pilots, raft and bear guides work for tips like anywhere else — 15-20% is normal for a good day. Bring cash; card readers don't work in the bush.

gotchas.title: Interior summers get smoky
gotchas.body: Wildfire season (late June-Aug) can bury Fairbanks and the Interior in smoke, grounding flightseeing and hiding Denali. Watch the forecast; keep plans flexible.

gotchas.title: Flightseeing weather refunds vary
gotchas.body: If clouds cover Denali, some operators fly a lower route, some refund, some rebook. Ask the cancellation and mountain-visibility policy before you pay.

gotchas.title: Layers, always layers
gotchas.body: One day can swing 40°F and rain sideways then clear. Waterproof shell, fleece, warm hat and real footwear go in the day pack every day, even in July.

gotchas.title: RV rentals book out by winter
gotchas.body: Summer motorhomes are snapped up months ahead and cost $200-350/night plus fuel and dump fees. Reserve early or you're priced out of the fleet that's left.

gotchas.title: Peak season is a hard wall
gotchas.body: Mid-June to mid-August is when lodging, cars, cruises and tours fill and prices peak. Late May and September are quieter and cheaper with most things still open.

gotchas.title: Some tribal lands need permission
gotchas.body: Roads and trails around Native villages may cross corporation land requiring a permit. Ask locally before you cross fences or camp near a village.

costs.note: Lunch + national-park narration usually included.
Summer; book 2-3 months out.
Half that in shoulder season; SUVs cost more.
Add studded tires; much cheaper than summer.
Plus fuel (poor mpg) and dump-station fees.
Summer peak; less shoulder season.

costs.item: Hotel / night, small town in July
costs.note: Seward, Homer, Talkeetna book out — reserve early.
Jet to King Salmon + float plane round trip.
From Talkeetna; ~1.5-2h, ski-plane landing on the glacier.
Shorter loop around the mountain.
Land on the glacier and mush the dog team.
Heated cabin, hot drinks, a photographer to shoot you under it.
Short winter run; glacier-landing mush costs far more.
Homer or Seward; often includes gear + processing extra.
Green camp/transit bus; road only runs to Mile 43 now.
7-day pass; many Alaska parks are free.
Coastal Classic, one way; Denali Star runs more.
Foot passenger; cabins + vehicles add a lot.
Coldfoot + the bush are the priciest; town is cheaper.
Halibut or king crab runs higher; groceries are pricey too.
Market price; Juneau + Anchorage crab shacks.
Matanuska or Root Glacier with crampons + guide.
Must pre-book 24h+ for the oilfield security escort.
1-day to annual; king stamp extra.

myths.reality: The capital has no road out. Juneau, Sitka, Ketchikan, Kodiak, Nome and Cordova are all air or ferry only.
The mountain makes its own weather and hides ~2 days in 3. Give yourself several days near it to improve the odds.

myths.claim: You need a cruise to see Alaska
myths.reality: A cruise shows you one strip of Southeast. The Interior, Denali, the Kenai and the Arctic need a car, train or bush plane.

myths.claim: Alaska is warm and sunny in summer
myths.reality: Coastal Southeast and the Kenai are cool and wet; 55-65°F and rain is normal. Pack a real rain shell even in July.
It's a fifth the size of the Lower 48. Anchorage to Fairbanks is a 7-hour drive; Nome to Anchorage isn't drivable at all.

myths.claim: You'll see the aurora in summer
myths.reality: The sky never gets dark enough June-July. Aurora season is roughly late August to mid-April.
They're common but shy. Make noise, store food, carry spray, keep your distance and encounters stay uneventful.
The road system, towns and cruise ports are developed and busy in summer. True backcountry starts where the roads end — which is most of the map.

myths.claim: Alaska is one time zone with the Lower 48
myths.reality: Almost the whole state is Alaska Time — an hour behind Pacific, four behind Eastern. Book flights carefully.

myths.claim: Alaska is cheap once you're there
myths.reality: Remote logistics make it one of the pricier US trips. Bush flights, ferries, summer hotels and fuel add up fast — budget accordingly.
Only above the Arctic Circle gets true midnight sun. Anchorage gets ~19 hours in June — long, but the sun does dip.

myths.claim: You can just show up and do Denali
myths.reality: Park buses, Brooks Falls, ferries and summer lodging sell out months ahead. The big-ticket items need reservations, not walk-ups.
